All right, welcome back. So hopefully you did that admittedly strange exercise, especially if you're doing it as your own consultant. But again, you know, super important because like why would we ever start to build a building if we don't have the plans for the building first, right? So in this next worksheet, what I want to take you through is finding all of the raw materials that you have at your disposal that we can use to get you money as soon as humanly possible. And that is the A part of Sandor, which stands for assets. Now again, remember, when I'm showing you this stuff, when you go through the actual presentation, I'm showing it to you within the context of this is what you would do for a client. But please, you can do the exact same thing for your own business. You can be your own client. I do it every quarter at minimum. I go through all of the very same things I'm showing you how to do right now. So our mission and this first part of certification is to get you some money. And what we're going to do right now is we're going to go through the A part of Sandor so you can find every bit of raw material you have at your disposal to actually make that happen. Ready? Let's go. So let's discuss this whole issue of assets. You probably already know this, but when I use the term assets, I'm not talking about financial assets that they have in the bank or whatever, and that might weird somebody out, you know, if you were to ask them about that. When I talk about assets in this particular case, all we're really looking to do is just list every imaginable thing that you and your client have at your disposal to work with so you can hit the target, right? And here's what's really cool about this. It's very, very rare for a client to fully realize the power of everything they have at their disposal. Usually what happens is a client will start to establish a pattern and they'll start focusing on only a couple of products and a couple of processes and the rest of their focus goes to problems. So they rarely understand that like all the stuff they have at their disposal that could be used. And what's interesting, and I'll kind of show you this in a minute, is that once you get all the assets out in the open, a lot of times you're going to get a strong idea of exactly what you need to do to hit the target. Like in many cases, the process you're about to go through will already tell you what to do to hit the target. And then you just validate that idea by getting the numbers and doing the diagnosis. So this extraction of the assets is very, very important. And that's why you need an asset checklist tool to make sure that you don't overlook anything. And of course, I've provided this tool to you. And I'm going to show it to you and we're going to go over it. So there's three main categories of assets we can use for our type of work. And I thought about like calling it ammunition instead, but it really doesn't sound right. I mean, these things really are assets to the business. So the first one is human assets. And these are customer lists, audiences, joint venture relationships, staff that can be used to do some work that you can charge money for, stuff like that, that involves people. The next category is transactional. And that's the traffic coming into your website, sales processes, funnels, campaigns, ads, stuff like that. And the third category is sellable. And that's like the things that you can sell products or services. And this is important. We also want to examine products that could be created. All right. So therefore I have createable. And for whatever reason, I think createable should not have a hyphen, but my auto correct insisted on it. So if it's not a hyphenable word, then blame Apple. Okay, so these are basically your three categories of assets. So clients usually don't realize how much they've got. So it's up to us to drag it out of them. And that's when this little sheet comes in handy. All right. Now, this isn't a sheet that you have to hand to a client. By the way, this is just something for your use. And you can just be jotting down notes as you're talking to the client. And in most cases, you're probably going to be talking to the client on the phone or on zoom or whatever. So it's not like you have to be belly to belly with them going through this. But this kind of makes everything organized and easy for you and me to kind of keep up with everything. Right. And you'll notice these three little categories here. And by the way, there's just so much gold in these right here. But we're going to go through the top three first. Okay, so human asset categories feels kind of weird saying human assets. But I don't really know how else to describe it, right? These are assets that involve people doing stuff. So here's your checklist. Number one, email prospect database. Number two, email customer database. Number three, Facebook engaged audience. Now, I'm going to make another video for this week to show you how to find this, by the way, because this is important. Number two, Instagram engaged audience. Now you'll notice I'm not putting Facebook fans or Instagram followers. And the reason why is let's say I got a million Facebook fans. Well, you know, 990,000 of those people could have become followers or fans like 10 years ago. The only the ones that are actually paying attention count as an asset. Yeah. Monthly unique podcast downloads. Important. You know, you can sell stuff on those podcasts, you can insert ads directly into a podcast spontaneously, like I could record an ad for anything right now, and call up Paul Colligan, who runs my podcast stuff and say, Hey, dynamically insert this in every podcast I've ever had ever. And they can just make that magic happen. So if you got a million monthly podcast downloads, and you want to start selling something, you can immediately insert ads for your own stuff in your own podcasts and switch them out and all kinds of cool stuff. Right. So we want to know that how many YouTube subscribers they have, kind of relevant, how many YouTube engaged viewers they have. This is relevant. And basically you find these two or these three metrics here through the platform. And so I'll show you how to do it in a minute. But you're like, Hey, YouTube, show me how many people have actually watched my stuff over the past 30 days. Right. And that's basically what we mean. That's what an engaged audience means. Website visitor retargeting audience. Big deal, by the way, real big deal. Okay. And relevant staff. Right. And so this is important, you know, because let's say that the client is like, Yeah, I'm a service provider and I do coaching and I'm at max capacity what have you discovered that they've had an assistant that's worked with them for the past 15 years that could do the their exact same job? Well, I mean, like the one thing would be, you know, okay, let's start seeing if we can book some clients that your assistant does the fulfillment on. So this is why we want to get relevant staff. Right. Do you have salespeople, all that kind of stuff. So this is your checklist of stuff to go through. I don't recommend you hand this checklist to the client. I recommend you actually have the conversation with the client, because if you hand the checklist to the client, they may might never actually give you anything back and be they're not going to know a lot of this. So you got to help them find it. So I'll give you a video in a minute to show you how to help them find some of these things here. All right. And so now we move over to transactional assets. Okay. And this is stuff like monthly website visits. And I'm considering this transactional instead of human because it's not necessarily something we like good broadcast to. However, it's important to know, right? Like what if we discover they're getting a million hits to their website every month and the website's got like no opt-in form, no call to action. And it's just horrible. Well, good chance we can just fix that one thing and hit the target or at least move pretty close to it, you know, sales funnels past and present. Okay. So this is something that you're probably going to have to grill them on. All right. Which is why we've got all this stuff down here, by the way, because they're focused on today and yesterday and worried about tomorrow. And rarely do they ever like remember stuff that worked really, really well. Ad campaigns past and present. So the cool thing about the internet doesn't really matter if you're using click funnels or infusion soft or whatever, like all this historical data can be gotten just by logging in and looking. So like if you have someone that's advertising on Facebook, you can literally be like, okay, let's pull up, you know, lifetime for this campaign. And then let's just look at every single month for this campaign and see if there was any particular month that was amazing. You know, or let's look at all the campaigns for the past year and see if any of them worked real good. And like, you don't actually have to do anything special to do that, you know, and that's kind of awesome. All right, so ad campaigns past and present really, really good. All right. Email sequences past and present. Sales processes past and present. Now, you might be wondering, hey, what's the difference between a sales funnel and a sales process? Well, a sales funnel is, hey, they opt in and they get all these emails and they buy the stuff. A sales process typically involves people. Right. So it might be opt in, see a webinar, sales person calls them, makes an appointment. Does demonstration does follow up, et cetera. And notice I keep saying past and present. A huge thing that happens to entrepreneurs myself included, by the way, and clients and like, I want to say every single client ever with very few exceptions. Trying to think of two, like Brooke Castillo never made this mistake. Carl White never made this mistake to my knowledge. I think those are the only two so far. Most clients have had stuff that worked real, real good. And they just stopped doing it. It's weird to God that they just get distracted because they like, you know, we're entrepreneurs and we like new stuff that we'll get bored with stuff that's working. So we'll be like, might as well stop doing that. That's working. Right. So we want to get that sales process is past and present. And now we get to the sellable assets. Right. So this means every product that is sellable. Okay. And by sellable, I mean that you have available and could conceivably sell if you wanted to. Right. So if you were to, if I were your client and you were doing this with me, I mean, I'd be listing a lot of stuff. Mass control, list control, mass conversion, you know, mass control, live event recordings, list control, live event recordings on high level fellowship, like, you know, it would, it'd be a long time. Right. But hey, guess what? When you get all this stuff out of somebody, the creative juices start flowing and it's like, hell, we could like run a campaign to sell that product tomorrow and probably get some money and move closer to our target. Right. So you want to get all that stuff out. You also want to get every service that is sellable. Right. So for example, if I wanted to, we could run ads. We could build funnels. We could write copy. We could produce video. We could script video. We could do all that kind of stuff if we wanted to. We don't necessarily have to, but we want to get it out. Right. Because we need every piece of ammunition we can get in order to hit the target. We don't have to use every asset, but we want to know what we've got available. OK. And then every product that is createable. All right. And this is important. And I'll illustrate it with a story. I've got a client, solid dude, Italian guy. So it's like internet marketing stuff in Italy makes a bunch of money. He has prided himself on only having one product. So he's a client in our private client group. And he's like, yeah, I have like maybe he has two products. I can't remember. But whatever. It's been a point of pride to have a very limited product selection. OK. And so he's like, how do I scale? And I'm like, well, you know, your your only answer is to get more customers because you only have these two things. And then we start talking about who the customers are. And I was assuming that the customers were largely beginners because his main products are really how to start an internet business and stuff. But oddly enough, the majority of the majority of his customers already own businesses. And so once we learned that, it was like, oh, my gosh, dude, like we can double your business by selling them more stuff because they all want to know, like how to get more customers in my existing business. So then we started making a list of all of the types of things that they needed to know, such as how to how to do a deadline promotion for a special sale, how to do a reactivation sequence for existing customers, how to run Facebook ads for a local business, you know, how to do a video sales letter, like all of this kind of stuff. He didn't have, but it was createable, you know, and it was certainly sellable. And then you want to do the same thing. I meant to write service there, every service that is createable. Sorry about that. So if there's a service that's createable, like, for example, I know that right now I could create a one on one coaching program that's not fulfilled by me, that does the Sandor process for people. And I know this because every time I do a small group, Sandor training, it sells out immediately and there's not even a sales letter for it. Right. And so we could easily create a process and a product to sell that and probably will, which is part of the reason I'm doing certifications. Because maybe some of the people who get certified can help fulfill that. Right. So that is a service that is createable, that does not yet exist. Right. So this is like your brainstorming with your your client here. Clients love this, by the way, because they're thinking of new stuff. And it's cool. Right. And another reason we want to do this is because a lot of times you'll find that the client doesn't have something like a one click up sell or an order bump or any sort of back end. And like, we know that's going to be part of the one thing they do. So you're already brainstorming on what to do here. All right. And so now we move down here. Okay. And we're kind of getting numbers from them here. Now, this isn't necessarily the numbers stage. We're still in the asset getting stage, but there's just so much gold here. All right. So I want you now, remember the targets or the clients you're working with are sweet spot clients. Right. Half million to five million and up in revenue. So if you were to ask this to a startup, it wouldn't really be that relevant. But keep in mind, the folks you're working with, they like, they got a lot of stuff going on and that's really, really good. So three big questions about past revenue that I want you to ask them. What was your biggest month of the past 12 months? Right. Now, here's the cool thing about the internet. Right. All of this stuff is just a simple log in and mouse click away. You know, so they've either got this already in their reporting or they can just log in to a stripe or authorize.net or infusion soft or whatever it is and literally see, oh, well, some bitch, it happened to have been July of 2019. And then you want to do your second biggest month and then you want to get your third. Right. And now what we want to do is we want to start reverse engineering. Okay. We want to be like, all right, what products we're selling in the biggest month. Right. So if the biggest month of the year of the last year was like September, now what we want to do is we want to have them log into their shopping cart system. I mean, like, okay, what the hell were you selling in September? What was going on? You know, and you're going to find out like, hey, we were selling these four things or three things or two things or whatever it was. Okay. And we want to do it for the second biggest month. And we want to do it for the third biggest month. And now we want to do a little bit more reverse engineering. And we want to find out, okay, what ads and promos ran during that biggest month. And by promos, by the way, I mean like internal promos. Okay. And then we want to find out the same for the second biggest month and the same for the third biggest month. And this stuff right here, by the way, like I kid you not, and a huge amount of cases, what's going to happen here is you're going to find out that they did something during this big month and sold some stuff with a particular ad and promo and then stopped doing it. And your job is going to be to get them to start doing it again and to keep doing it. And thankfully, it's actually not really that hard. And when I tell you like the overwhelming majority of the time, this is what happens, I literally mean, I'm thinking right now of one client who has had repeatedly had big months and during those months, the big revenue driver was the client did live webinars. And so I'm like, our last meeting, this was in a private client situation and not in an accelerator situation. Our last meeting, I was like, hey, why don't you do like three more of those live webinars and the client did and what do you know, more money was made. So now it's like, hey, why don't we record the webinars and do them as on-demand classes, you know, and the client's like, yeah, OK, let's do that. And so clients, no dummy, by the way, is just that they're busy doing the business stuff. Yeah, I've got another client. I'll tell you about right now. No kidding. All right. Dug into this stuff, found out that during one of the big months, they were running ads on Facebook that were lead generation ads. And the very first email that was sent out for every person who opted in generated like $2.81 per email sent. That's just an email number one during the post-opt-in sequence. The ad campaigns got something like an 11 to one return on investment. Guess what ad campaigns are not running? Those ad campaigns. So what do you think the one thing was here? Now, like we haven't even gotten into hardcore numbers yet, right? Like I'm just asking about stuff that worked real good. What do you think the one thing was? Turn them some bitches back on a right and B, why don't we set up a little automation and look and for everyone who didn't click on the link in email number one or everyone who didn't open email number one and that little welcome sequence, let's send them the same freaking email again. You know, I know it sounds dumb and the clients are not dumb. This client that I'm specifically referencing did more volume in sales than I did. So as they're not dumb people, it's just people can't see the forest through the trees because they're too busy putting out fires. That's why we want to do this kind of stuff. All right, so here's what I'm going to do for you. First of all, I'm going to advise you to go and just get with people and practice this on them for nothing. OK, like literally go up to anybody, like if you're in Facebook groups or whatever, you can even call this like a revenue finding exercise. Be like, hey, listen, I've got this process that I want to practice on you with. I need some volunteers. You need to be doing at least half a million plus in your business. And I want to take you through a process where we can find ways to immediately increase revenue and I'm doing this for free because I want to practice. And you'll get takers, right? Like any private groups you're part of, whatever. Just practice on some people. And of course, you're going to be practicing with your instructor during the class and all of this. But I'm telling you, when you do a couple of these in real life, you're going to be like, oh, my gosh, you've got to be freaking kidding me because these three last columns here are what shows you what to do. You know, typically that's what happens, right? But not always. OK, not always.
